reasoning: The finding is not a real broken access control vulnerability. The gate at class-main.php line 88 — `is_admin() && current_user_can('publish_posts') && Common::user_has_allowed_role()` — functions as designed. `user_has_allowed_role()` (class-common.php lines 60-80) merges the hardcoded list [administrator, shop_manager] with `$settings['article']['allowed_roles']`, which DEFAULTS to an empty array (class-main.php line 224). Editors and authors are only included when a site administrator explicitly enables them in plugin settings — this is deliberate trust delegation, not a capability bypass. The claim that 'widened role assignment in settings directly enables stored code injection' is factually accurate as a feature description, but the code does not bypass any access control: only an administrator (manage_options) can change the allowed_roles setting (class-main.php line 90-91, Settings constructor gated on manage_options). The save handlers in Metabox_Article (line 126) and Metabox_Taxonomy (line 124) check `edit_post` / `edit_terms` — appropriate capabilities for their context. Both handlers call `Common::sanitize_hfc_data()` which applies `sanitize_html_with_scripts()`: this runs wp_kses() on the HTML structure and sanitizes opening tags of script/style blocks (preserving inner JS/CSS content by design, since this plugin's core purpose is code injection). The plugin also explicitly shows a WARNING security notice to users when they use the code-injection fields. The finding misframes a configurable, admin-controlled trust delegation as a broken access control issue. There is no code path through which a non-admin can enable these roles for themselves, and the default configuration exposes no elevated access to editors or authors. This does not meet the bar for a reportable broken access control vulnerability.
